document.title: Classmates We Remember
document.subtitle: UCLA Anderson MBA — Class of 1996
document.description: Best-of merge across two independent research passes — preferring published obituaries over drafted ones, with all photographs aggregated.
source_files: Analytical Report on Seven Deceased Individuals from the Memorial Page.docx

died.notes: An earlier LA Times article (June 11) called the day 'Friday', which would imply June 7. Reported age also conflicts (31 vs 32). June 6 / age 31 is the best-supported reading.

dates.buried: Pacific View Memorial Park, Corona del Mar, Orange County, CA (Seaview, Lot 1453, Space D)

education.notes: Athletic scholarship; football team — Rose Bowl and Fiesta Bowl teams

career.employer: Mater Dei High School, Santa Ana, CA
career.role: English Teacher and Assistant Football Coach
Investment Banking (offer accepted, never started — death occurred before start date)

classmates.biography: Greg grew up in Fountain Valley, California, where he was a first-team all-county high school tight end. He earned an athletic scholarship to UCLA, played on Rose Bowl and Fiesta Bowl teams, and graduated in 1987 with a B.A. in English. He went on to earn a secondary teaching credential from CSU Long Beach in 1990, then taught English and served as an assistant football coach at Mater Dei High School in Santa Ana. He returned to UCLA for his MBA at the Anderson School of Management. At the time of his death he was completing the program and had accepted an investment-banking role with Lehman Brothers in San Francisco. He died on June 6, 1996, age 31, killed riding his bicycle. UCLA Anderson awarded him his MBA posthumously.
classmates.memorial_legacy: In 2006 — ten years after his death — UCLA Anderson established the Greg Bolin Fellowship, supporting MBA students in his memory.

obituary.source: Synthesized from LA Times reports (June 11 and June 15, 1996) and UCLA Anderson institutional sources
obituary.text: Gregory P. "Greg" Bolin, 31, of Fountain Valley, California, was killed in a bicycle accident on Thursday, June 6, 1996, weeks short of completing his MBA at the UCLA Anderson School of Management. A 1987 graduate of UCLA — where he played on Rose Bowl and Fiesta Bowl football teams after a standout high-school career as a first-team all-county tight end — Greg returned to teaching after college, earning a secondary credential from CSU Long Beach in 1990 and joining Mater Dei High School in Santa Ana as an English teacher and assistant football coach. He had recently accepted an investment-banking position with Lehman Brothers in San Francisco. Greg was an athlete's athlete and a teacher's teacher: loyal, hard-working, and generous with his time. He is buried at Pacific View Memorial Park in Corona del Mar. UCLA Anderson awarded him his MBA posthumously, and in 2006 his classmates and family established the Greg Bolin Fellowship at Anderson in his memory.

career.notes: Described by Leaf Group as a 20-year e-commerce and marketing veteran

classmates.biography: Andrea earned a B.S. in Business Administration from Pepperdine in 1988, then worked as an institutional specialty pharmaceutical representative at Pfizer in Los Angeles before entering UCLA Anderson, where she earned her MBA in 1996. Over the next two decades she became a notable Los Angeles consumer-brand and digital-commerce executive, holding senior roles across pharmaceuticals, e-commerce, editorial design, and consumer brands. She served on the Los Angeles board of Camp Kesem, the national nonprofit supporting children of parents with cancer.
classmates.memorial_legacy: A Project Purple memorial fund was created in her honor to support pancreatic-cancer research and patient outcomes.

family.father: John William Borda (1933–2020), Gardnerville/Carson City, NV

obituary.source: Synthesized from family Instagram announcement (Aug 14, 2021) and Project Purple memorial fund language
obituary.text: Andrea E. (Borda) Stanford, of Los Angeles, California, died on August 14, 2021, after a battle with pancreatic cancer. A graduate of Pepperdine University (B.S. 1988) and the UCLA Anderson School of Management (M.B.A. 1996), Andrea was a brand strategist and creative executive whose 20-year career bridged editorial design, e-commerce, and consumer-brand leadership. After early years at Pfizer and at eStyle/Babystyle.com, she held senior roles at the Walt Disney Company, helped launch C Magazine, and went on to serve as VP of Merchandising and General Manager of Hunters Alley at One Kings Lane and as SVP of Marketing & Partnerships at Everything But The House. In 2018 she was named Senior Vice President and General Manager of Society6 by Leaf Group. She was a board member of Camp Kesem-Los Angeles. Andrea is survived by her husband, Steven Stanford, and her three children — Ryan, Brooke, and Jade — and was preceded in death by her father, John W. Borda. A Project Purple memorial fund was created in her honor to advance pancreatic-cancer research. Andrea was known for her warmth, her impeccable taste, and her ability to build teams and brands with equal grace.

classmates.biography: Jeff was raised in St. Joseph, Michigan, the son of Dr. Gary C. Lulenski. He graduated from Michigan State University in 1990, then joined SAS Institute as an account representative based in Chicago. He met his wife Heather and later moved to California, ultimately settling in San Jose. After earning his UCLA Anderson MBA in 1996, Jeff built a 25-year career in business development across media and digital — at Deloitte, Tribune, Knight Ridder Digital, Yahoo!, Manilla, hibu, and GoDaddy.

obituary.status: Indexed in The Herald-Palladium ('Obituary for Jeffrey Steven Lulenski, 1967-2020') but text not retrievable on the open web
obituary.source: Synthesized from Michigan State in memoriam, Herald-Palladium index, family obituaries (Starks Family Funeral Homes), and BIA Leading In Local 2013 attendee list
obituary.text: Jeffrey Steven "Jeff" Lulenski, of San Jose, California, died on April 24, 2020, at age 52. A graduate of Michigan State University (B.A. 1990) and the UCLA Anderson School of Management (M.B.A. 1996), Jeff spent his career building business-development and partnership organizations across the technology and media industries — at Deloitte, Tribune, Knight Ridder Digital, Yahoo!, Manilla, hibu, and GoDaddy. He was a music and film lover and a sports enthusiast. He is survived by his wife Heather and daughters Sienna and Kelsey, and by his brothers Michael and David. His stepmother Kathryn ('Kaye') Lulenski passed in 2022; his father Dr. Gary C. Lulenski passed in 2025. Friends and colleagues remember Jeff for his easygoing humor, his loyalty, and his quiet competence — the kind of partner who made every team and every deal he touched better.

career.role: Editor of the Editorial Pages / Political Editor

career.notes: Final role at death; helped build Votorantim's new-business arm; final transaction was the 2010 merger of Optiglobe and Proceda to form Tivit

scholarship.title: Institutional Sources of Corruption in Brazil
scholarship.reprinted_in: Corruption and Political Reform in Brazil: The Impact of Collor's Impeachment (1999, North-South Center Press, University of Miami)
scholarship.significance: Frequently cited foundational text on Brazilian political institutions; argues that the 1988 Constitution's institutional design produced systemic cronyism

classmates.biography: Born and raised in São Paulo, Brazil, Artur earned a B.A. in Journalism from the University of São Paulo in 1984. He joined Folha de São Paulo and rose to become Editor of the Editorial Pages. In the late 1980s he came to UCLA for graduate study in Political Science, earning his Ph.D. in 1994 under Professor Barbara Geddes, then his MBA from UCLA Anderson in 1996. He returned to Brazil and worked as a consultant at Booz Allen & Hamilton before joining the Votorantim Group as Executive Director of Votorantim Venture Capital. His most prominent transaction was the 2010 merger of Optiglobe and Proceda to form Tivit. Artur lived at the rare intersection of journalism, political-science scholarship, and finance.

family.spouse: Angela Issler Marsiaj — Brazilian writer and novelist; born in Porto Alegre, raised in São Paulo. Author of the novels Soroca and O enigma de todos os tempos (Editora Urutau).

obituary.title: Morre o executivo Artur Ribeiro, diretor do grupo Votorantim
obituary.excerpt_translated: He died of cancer in the early hours of Tuesday, September 20, at Hospital Sírio Libanês, in São Paulo, at age 46. Artur Ribeiro Neto, an administrator and journalist, was Executive Director of Votorantim Venture Capital (VVC), the new-business area of the Votorantim Group. He was a graduate in journalism from the School of Communications and Arts of USP, a career path he followed before transitioning into business and finance.

tribute_quote.speaker: Paulo Henrique de Oliveira Santos
tribute_quote.text: Artur is the one who helped me build, practically from scratch, the new-business area of the Votorantim Group. What always characterized him was the consistency of his brilliant insights.

classmates.biography: Randy grew up in Pacific Palisades, California. He graduated from the Thacher School in Ojai in 1987, deferred college for a year of study abroad in Finland, and then earned his B.A. in History from Georgetown University in 1992, where he was active in theater and met his future wife, Karen Dahl. He worked as a legal assistant at Wilmer, Cutler & Pickering in Washington, DC before entering UCLA Anderson, earning his MBA in 1996. Randy spent more than 12 years at Microsoft in Redmond, then served as VP of Product Management at DTS, and in 2017 became VP of Corporate and Business Development at Syntonic. He was diagnosed with cancer at age 49 and continued working and living life fully alongside his treatment for nearly four years.

obituary.excerpt: Randy Smith, a beloved husband and father with a wry sense of humor and an impressive career in product development and strategy for tech companies, died peacefully September 4 at age of 52 at his home in Bellevue, Washington, due to cancer. Randy grew up in Pacific Palisades, California and graduated from Thacher School in Ojai in 1987. He deferred a year of college to do study abroad in Finland and then returned to begin at Georgetown University where he majored in history and was active in theater. After earning his BA 1992, he earned his MBA from Anderson School of Business at UCLA.
obituary.celebration_of_life: October 23, 2021, at St. Matthew's Church, Pacific Palisades, CA

career.employer: San Francisco Museum of Modern Art

classmates.biography: Stefanie was born April 9, 1967 in Baltimore and raised in Tampa, the daughter of Dr. Barry S. Verkauf — a pioneering Tampa reproductive endocrinologist who performed Florida's first successful IVF birth — and Arline Verkauf. She graduated from Berkeley Preparatory School in Tampa, then earned a B.A. in Theater Arts from the University of Pennsylvania in 1989. Before UCLA Anderson, she was Associate Director of Development at Playwrights Horizons in New York. After earning her MBA in 1996, Stefanie applied her arts-and-fundraising background in San Francisco, working in development at the San Francisco Ballet and the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art. She later married Michael Costello and the family moved to Miami.
classmates.memorial_legacy: UCLA Anderson maintains the Stefanie L. Verkauf Memorial Fellowship, supporting MBA students in her memory.

publications: Van Orsdel funeral home memorial page
obituary.summary: Stefanie was remembered as creative and always interested in the performing and visual arts and their value to society. The Tampa Bay Times described her life as 'a life half finished.' She worked for the San Francisco Ballet and the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art before relocating to Miami after marrying Michael Costello. The family asked that donations be made in her memory to Voices Against Brain Cancer.
obituary.memorial_service: December 12, 2010, 1:30 p.m., Congregation Rodeph Sholom, 2713 Bayshore Boulevard, Tampa

career.role: Heirloom tomato grower / commercial business builder

classmates.biography: Paul came from a military lineage — his father, Col. Donovan Paul Yeuell Jr. (USMA 1940), is buried at West Point, and his grandfather Col. Donovan Paul Yeuell Sr. (1894–1953) preceded them both. Paul broke from that path and graduated from Stanford in 1974 with a B.A. in American Literature, where he was co-captain of the lacrosse team. He spent many years in Los Angeles as a working actor and writer. He returned to UCLA Anderson and earned his MBA in 1996. In 2018, at age 67, he reinvented himself again, moving to Mount Vernon, Washington in the Skagit Valley to build a commercial heirloom tomato business. He was an ocean swimmer and paddler, a mountaineer, and devoted to his Belgian Tervuren, Harley.

family.father: Col. Donovan Paul Yeuell Jr., USMA 1940 (predeceased; buried West Point)

obituary.verbatim_text: Donovan Paul Yeuell III, '74 (English), of Mount Vernon, Wash., December 20, at 70. He was co-captain of the lacrosse team and went on to earn an MBA from UCLA. He was a master storyteller who spent many years in Los Angeles as an actor and writer. In 2018, he dedicated himself to building a commercial heirloom tomato business. He never missed an opportunity to swim or paddle in the ocean. He loved his Belgian Tervuren named Harley. Survivors include his sisters, Sherry Craven, Susan, and Michel.

methodology.rule_for_obituary: Prefer published obituary over drafted; use verbatim where reasonable, summarize where copyright concerns apply; draft from sourced facts where no published obituary exists.
methodology.rule_for_photos: Aggregate all photos located across both research efforts. Yearbook portraits are baseline; modern portraits added where available.

confidence_levels.HIGH: Confirmed in a primary source (Stanford Magazine, Tampa Bay Times, LA Times, Seattle Times, Exame, Van Orsdel funeral home, MSU institutional in memoriam, Find A Grave headstone)
confidence_levels.MEDIUM: Confirmed in a single secondary or institutional source not corroborated elsewhere
confidence_levels.LOW: Inferred from indirect facts (e.g., graduation year → approximate birth year)
